question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 1
A 65-year-old woman comes to the physician for a follow-up examination
after blood pressure measurements were
175/105 mm Hg and 185/110 mm Hg 1 and 3 weeks ago, respectively. She
has well-controlled type 2 diabetes
mellitus. Her blood pressure now is 175/110 mm Hg. Physical examination
shows no other abnormalities.
Antihypertensive therapy is started, but her blood pressure remains
elevated at her next visit 3 weeks later. Laboratory
studies show increased plasma renin activity; the erythrocyte
sedimentation rate and serum electrolytes are within the
reference ranges. Angiography shows a high-grade stenosis of the proximal
right renal artery; the left renal artery
appears normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Atherosclerosis.
Atherosclerotic renovascular disease is the most likely cause given the patient’s
age, cardiovascular risk (type 2 diabetes), high-grade proximal renal artery
stenosis, resistant hypertension with elevated renin, and normal ESR. In older
adults, atherosclerosis is the predominant etiology of renal artery stenosis and
typically involves ostial/proximal segments of the main renal artery, often
unilaterally, and presents with renovascular hypertension that is renin mediated.
[1][2][3][4][5] The American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association
guideline emphasizes renovascular disease as a key cause of secondary
hypertension in resistant cases, with atherosclerosis most common in this
demographic; the American Heart Association scientific statement underscores
that atherosclerotic renovascular disease predominates in patients >65 years and
affects large proximal renal arteries.[1][2] Epidemiologic series demonstrate
atherosclerosis as the leading cause of renal artery stenosis in patients >40
years.[4] Fibromuscular dysplasia typically affects younger women, involves
distal/branch segments, and often shows a string-of-beads pattern rather than
isolated proximal stenosis.[2][6][7] Large-vessel vasculitides (Takayasu, temporal
arteritis) are far less likely at age 65 and usually feature elevated inflammatory
markers; normal ESR here argues against them.[4][7][8] Congenital hypoplasia is
uncommon and would not preferentially present with new-onset resistant
hypertension at this age.
Therefore, the best answer is A. Atherosclerosis.[1][2][3][4][5]
references: Revascularization for Renovascular Disease: A Scientific Statement From
Renovascular Hypertension. Herrmann SM, Textor SC.. Endocrinology and
Fibromuscular Dysplasia: State of the Science and Critical Unanswered

question:
(MC-NJ) - Step 2
A 23-year-old man who is on active duty in the US Army comes to the clinic
1 hour after sustaining a bee sting to
his right cheek. The patient reports continued pain at the site of the sting,
but he has not had shortness of breath,
throat tightness, loss of vision, or other neurologic symptoms. Medical
history is unremarkable. He takes no
medications. The patient has no known allergies. He is 178 cm (5 ft 10 in) tall
and weighs 64 kg (140 lb); BMI is 20
kg/m². Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 86/min, respirations are 10/
min, and blood pressure is
120/70 mm Hg. Examination of the right cheek shows an erythematous,
swollen area of induration surrounding a
central clear area with the stinger in situ. The swelling encroaches on the
lower aspect of the patient's right orbit,
which appears puffy and mildly dusky. After the stinger is removed, which
of the following is the most appropriate
next step in management?

options.B: Admit the patient to the hospital for intravenous vancomycin therapy
options.C: Cleanse the site and apply ice

explanation:
Cleanse the site and apply ice
This presentation is consistent with a large local reaction to a Hymenoptera
sting without systemic manifestations or ocular injury. After prompt stinger
removal, symptomatic care is recommended: cleanse the site, apply cold/ice to
reduce pain and edema, and consider oral analgesics; oral antihistamines may
help pruritus if present.[1][2] The American Heart Association/American Red
Cross First Aid Guidelines support topical ice/cold packs for pain and swelling
after bee/wasp stings, emphasizing rapid stinger removal to limit venom
delivery.[1] The American Academy of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology practice
parameter advises cold compresses and symptomatic therapy for large local
reactions; routine antibiotics are not indicated unless clear evidence of
secondary infection exists, as swelling/lymphangitis can be mediator-driven.[2]
Reviews similarly caution against unnecessary antibiotics or epinephrine in
isolated large local reactions.[3]
Epinephrine is reserved for anaphylaxis, which is not present. [1][2]
Hospital admission/IV vancomycin or oral clindamycin are inappropriate
without clinical cellulitis or systemic illness. [2][4]
Ophthalmology consultation is warranted for direct ocular involvement
(corneal sting, vision changes, severe ocular findings), not simple periorbital
extension of local edema from a facial sting.[1][5][6][7]
references: Stinging Insect Hypersensitivity: A Practice Parameter Update 2016.
